solutions.solution:
This is a 'network' set. Begin by drawing the diagram: All normal requests are received by either M or N, and all priority requests are received by M or Q:
NORMAL    M
          N
PRIORITY  M
          Q
All requests processed by M are then sent to T:
NORMAL  M → T
PRIORITY M → T
All requests processed by N are sent to S:
NORMAL  N → S
PRIORITY N → S
All requests processed by T are then sent to P; priority requests processed by T are then sent to Q:
NORMAL  M → T → P
        N → S
PRIORITY M → T
         Q
Normal requests processed by S are then sent either to T or P:
NORMAL  M → T → P
        N → S
PRIORITY M → T
         Q
Normal requests processed by P are then sent to Q and priority requests processed by P are then sent to R:
NORMAL  M → T → P → Q
        N → S
PRIORITY M → T    R
         Q       P

The diagram shows that Q cannot send a request to Q, though he can send one to P, who in turn can send one to Q. The diagram also shows that Q can forward a request to P, who in turn can forward it to R. A normal request received by M must be processed by T, P and Q before it goes to R. A priority request received by M must go to both T and Q to get to R, so it is not part of the correct answer. If it is passed to R, Q can forward a priority request directly to R. Note the two shortcuts or routes in the normal requests are M, T, P and N, S, P, Q, R. In each case, a minimum of four stations must handle the request before it gets to R. A normal request can be shifted back and forth between P and Q any number of times. T, however, is not within such a loop. Everything R receives must at some point have passed through station Q, so it is not part of the correct answer. There are certain requests, however, that P does not handle, namely those priority requests forwarded by Q directly to R. Also, there are certain requests that M does not handle, namely those normal requests that begin with N and those priority requests that begin with Q. A normal request that enters at N proceeds to S and then to P (or five stations) before reaching R.

From (iv): B > D > F (intelligence)
From (v): A (intelligence) A > D (weight)
From (vi): F > B > C (weight)
From (vii): E = D (weight), D > F (weight)
These can be combined as:
B > D > F > A (intelligence)
A > D > F > B > C (weight)
Note that B is the more intelligent, but A is heavier than all except E. So, E must be the heaviest. Hence, we have:
E > A > D > F > B > C (weight)
So, E is the most intelligent. So, C must be the least intelligent. So we have:
most intelligent > A (intelligence)
C > B > D > F
